title: The relationship between business objectives and organisational structure
summary: 9609 A Level — why structure follows strategy, alignment with objectives, and consequences of mismatch.

simpleExplanation.title: Form follows purpose
simpleExplanation.summary: Organisational structure should enable business objectives — a cost-leadership strategy needs tight control; innovation objectives need flexible, cross-functional teams.
steps: Objectives determine required coordination and speed.
Structure defines roles, layers, and reporting lines.
Diversified firms often decentralise by division.
Start-ups stay flat until growth demands formal hierarchy.

flashcards.back: Chandler — org design should support strategic goals (6.2).
May need central control, standardisation, functional structure.
Flat/matrix structure, delegated authority, R&D autonomy.
Centralised HQ slowing local market response.
Informal structure becomes inadequate — need formal layers (7.1.2).
May need geographic divisions or matrix.
State objective → describe current structure → judge fit → recommend change.
Structure affects empowerment of managers and employee morale.

sections.content: **Objectives and structure** (7.1.1) opens **Topic 7**. Always tie **6.2 strategy** to **org chart** — Paper 3 loves **restructure recommendations**.

sections.question: Multinational adopts strategy of local product adaptation in each country but retains all marketing decisions at global HQ. Analyse the structure–objective fit.
sections.solution:
**Objective:** **Local adaptation** needs **fast local decisions** on pricing, promotion, packaging.

**Structure problem:** **Centralised marketing** creates **delays** and **generic campaigns** — misalignment.

**Consequences:** Lost market share, demotivated country managers (2.2), competitor wins with local offers.

**Fix:** **Decentralise marketing** to regional divisions (7.1.5) with **global brand guidelines** only — matrix or divisional structure.
